name: Epistemic Uncertainty
definition: Uncertainty caused by incomplete knowledge about the world, the data-generating process, or the model itself. In many settings it can be reduced through better evidence, improved models, or additional observation.
my_take: I use this as a reminder that confidence is partly a property of what we know, not only of what is true. When an AI-risk claim feels uncertain, I want to ask which missing evidence could actually narrow that uncertainty rather than treating uncertainty as one undifferentiated fog.
why_it_matters: Arguments about advanced AI often depend on sparse evidence, novel systems, and extrapolation beyond observed conditions. Distinguishing reducible uncertainty helps identify where research, evaluation, or monitoring could change the decision.

sources.title: Aleatoric and Epistemic Uncertainty in Machine Learning: An Introduction to Concepts and Methods

name: Aleatoric Uncertainty
definition: Uncertainty arising from randomness, noise, or variation inherent in the process being observed. More data may characterize it more precisely without eliminating it.
my_take: This distinction keeps me from assuming every unknown can eventually be solved with enough research. Some systems are genuinely stochastic, and some outcomes remain variable even when the mechanism is well understood.
why_it_matters: Safety decisions need to account for irreducible variation in model outputs, environments, users, and deployment conditions rather than treating one successful test as a guarantee of repeatable behavior.

sources.title: What Uncertainties Do We Need in Bayesian Deep Learning for Computer Vision?

name: Knightian Uncertainty
definition: Uncertainty for which reliable probabilities are unavailable or poorly defined, often because the situation is novel, the possible outcomes are not fully known, or the underlying model is contested.
my_take: I think this describes a large part of the advanced-AI debate better than ordinary risk does. Assigning a number can be useful, but a precise-looking probability does not create evidence that the situation itself does not support.
why_it_matters: Some transformative-AI questions involve unprecedented systems and poorly specified outcome spaces, so decision methods that assume stable probabilities may create false confidence.

sources.title: Risk, Uncertainty, and Profit
sources.importance: Foundational distinction between measurable risk and uncertainty

name: Calibration
definition: The degree to which stated confidence matches observed frequency. Among claims assigned 70 percent confidence, for example, a well-calibrated forecaster should be correct about 70 percent of the time.
my_take: I care about calibration because fluency and certainty are easy to confuse. A model, expert, or author can sound decisive while having no demonstrated relationship between confidence and accuracy.
why_it_matters: Risk assessment, model evaluation, and governance all depend on knowing whether confidence estimates can be trusted, especially when decisions are triggered by predicted probabilities.

sources.title: On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks
sources.authors: Chuan Guo, Geoff Pleiss, Yu Sun, Kilian Q. Weinberger

name: Bayesian Updating
definition: The process of revising beliefs by combining prior expectations with new evidence, producing a posterior belief that reflects both.
my_take: What I like about Bayesian reasoning is not that every belief needs a perfect numerical prior. It is the discipline of asking what I believed before, what the new evidence actually discriminates between, and how much movement that evidence deserves.
why_it_matters: AI capabilities and risks are changing quickly. A useful position should update when evidence arrives rather than treating early confidence as an identity to defend.

sources.title: Bayesian Data Analysis

name: Base Rates
definition: The background frequency of an event or outcome within an appropriate reference class before case-specific evidence is considered.
my_take: Base rates are difficult in AI because the most important claims are often framed as historically unique. I still want the outside view: how often do ambitious technical forecasts fail, how often do safeguards erode, and what happens in comparable high-stakes technologies?
why_it_matters: Ignoring background frequencies can make vivid scenarios, demonstrations, or anecdotes appear more predictive than they are.

sources.title: On the Psychology of Prediction
sources.importance: Foundational study of base-rate neglect and representativeness

name: Forecasting
definition: The practice of making explicit, testable predictions about future events, ideally with time bounds, probability estimates, resolution criteria, and a record that permits accuracy to be evaluated.
my_take: Forecasting forces vague confidence into a form that can eventually be wrong. I do not think every philosophical claim can be reduced to a forecast, but many capability and deployment claims become much clearer when someone has to specify what, when, and how likely.
why_it_matters: Transparent forecasts make disagreement measurable and allow institutions to learn which experts, methods, and indicators have actually been informative.

sources.title: Identifying and Cultivating Superforecasters as a Method of Improving Probabilistic Predictions

name: Expert Elicitation
definition: A structured process for obtaining and combining expert judgments when direct empirical evidence is incomplete, often by decomposing questions, eliciting probabilities, documenting assumptions, and testing sensitivity to disagreement.
my_take: Expert opinion is evidence, but it is not a magic category. I trust it more when the question is structured, uncertainty is elicited explicitly, dissent is preserved, and the process is designed to reduce status effects and group convergence.
why_it_matters: Many frontier-AI decisions must be made before large datasets exist, making the quality of expert judgment and aggregation methods unusually consequential.

sources.title: Uncertain Judgements: Eliciting Experts' Probabilities

name: Epistemic Humility
definition: The practice of recognizing the limits and fallibility of one's knowledge while remaining willing to hold justified beliefs and revise them in response to better evidence.
my_take: For me, humility is not performative uncertainty or refusing to take a position. It means being specific about why I believe something, where the inference is weakest, and what evidence would make me move.
why_it_matters: AI debates reward certainty, novelty, and strong narratives. Epistemic humility helps prevent confidence from outrunning evidence without turning uncertainty into paralysis.

sources.title: Predictors and Consequences of Intellectual Humility

name: Falsifiability
definition: The property of a claim being exposed to possible evidence that could count against it, rather than being protected from every conceivable outcome.
my_take: I keep coming back to this when an argument can reinterpret every success, failure, delay, or contradiction as support. Not every meaningful claim is cleanly falsifiable, but a theory should still tell me what observations would weaken it.
why_it_matters: AI-risk claims can become difficult to evaluate when they concern hidden objectives or distant futures. Explicit disconfirming evidence keeps the discussion connected to inquiry rather than only narrative coherence.

sources.title: The Logic of Scientific Discovery

name: Expected Value
definition: A decision criterion that weights each possible outcome by its probability and sums the resulting values, allowing uncertain options to be compared on their probability-weighted consequences.
my_take: Expected value is indispensable, but its outputs are only as good as the probabilities, outcome definitions, and value judgments that enter it. I become cautious when tiny, highly uncertain probabilities are multiplied by enormous consequences and presented as precise conclusions.
why_it_matters: Much of catastrophic-risk prioritization depends on combining low probabilities with very large stakes, so assumptions about probability and value can dominate the recommendation.

sources.title: Decision Theory

name: Tail Risk
definition: The possibility of outcomes in the extreme ends of a probability distribution, especially rare events with consequences far larger than ordinary variation.
my_take: Tail risk is where average performance stops being reassuring. A system can be useful almost all of the time and still be unacceptable if a small class of failures is irreversible, correlated, or capable of spreading across institutions.
why_it_matters: Catastrophic AI arguments focus less on typical outputs than on low-frequency pathways with unusually severe consequences.

sources.title: Statistical Consequences of Fat Tails

name: Fat-Tailed Distributions
definition: Probability distributions in which extreme outcomes occur more often, and contribute more to total risk, than they would under thin-tailed distributions such as the normal distribution.
my_take: I am careful about using 'fat-tailed' as a dramatic synonym for uncertainty. It is a statistical claim about distributional structure, and we often do not yet have enough observations to know which distribution describes advanced-AI outcomes.
why_it_matters: If relevant harms are fat-tailed, historical averages and ordinary stress tests may substantially underestimate the contribution of extreme events.

sources.title: The Variation of Certain Speculative Prices
sources.importance: Foundational empirical work on heavy-tailed behavior

name: Risk Asymmetry
definition: A decision situation in which the costs of false reassurance and false alarm are substantially different, so equal error rates do not imply equal consequences.
my_take: This is one reason I resist arguments that treat caution and inaction as mirror images. Overreacting can impose real costs, but underreacting may close off the ability to correct the mistake later. The asymmetry has to be argued, not merely asserted.
why_it_matters: AI policy often weighs uncertain restrictions against uncertain catastrophic harms, and those errors may differ sharply in reversibility, scale, and who bears them.

sources.title: Moral Uncertainty and Our Relationships with Unknown Minds
sources.importance: Applied analysis of ethical-epistemic risk asymmetries

name: Precautionary Principle
definition: The principle that plausible threats of serious or irreversible harm can justify preventive action even when scientific certainty about probability or mechanism is incomplete.
my_take: I see precaution as a burden-shifting framework, not a command to prohibit anything uncertain. It is strongest when the possible harm is severe, alternatives exist, safeguards are proportionate, and the intervention itself is also evaluated for harm.
why_it_matters: Advanced-AI governance may need to act before catastrophic pathways can be demonstrated empirically, because waiting for direct evidence could mean waiting until prevention is no longer possible.

sources.title: The Precautionary Principle
sources.authors: UNESCO World Commission on the Ethics of Scientific Knowledge and Technology

name: Robust Decision-Making
definition: A family of methods that searches for strategies that perform acceptably across many plausible futures rather than optimizing for one best-estimate forecast.
my_take: This is one of the most practical responses to deep uncertainty. I may not know which AI trajectory is correct, but I can still ask which policies preserve options, reveal information, and avoid failing catastrophically across several credible scenarios.
why_it_matters: AI governance must often choose under disagreement about timelines, capabilities, actors, and failure modes. Robust strategies can remain useful without requiring consensus on one forecast.

sources.title: Shaping the Next One Hundred Years: New Methods for Quantitative, Long-Term Policy Analysis
sources.authors: Robert J. Lempert, Steven W. Popper, Steven C. Bankes

name: Pascal's Mugging
definition: A decision-theory problem in which an extremely small or poorly grounded probability of an astronomically large payoff appears to dominate ordinary choices under straightforward expected-value reasoning.
my_take: I do not think the lesson is to ignore low-probability catastrophe. It is to notice when the size of the imagined outcome is doing all the work while the probability estimate is unconstrained, manipulable, or detached from evidence.
why_it_matters: Existential-risk arguments can become vulnerable to this structure when enormous stakes are paired with probabilities that cannot be meaningfully calibrated.

sources.title: Pascal's Mugging

name: Moral Uncertainty
definition: Uncertainty about which moral principles, theories, or values are correct and therefore about what one ought to do even when the empirical facts are known.
my_take: Alignment cannot escape moral uncertainty by asking for 'human values' as though they form one coherent target. I want the Atlas to preserve disagreement about values rather than hiding it inside a reward model or governance process.
why_it_matters: Decisions about AI objectives, acceptable risk, moral status, and distribution require action despite unresolved ethical disagreement.

sources.title: Moral Uncertainty
sources.authors: William MacAskill, Krister Bykvist, Toby Ord

name: Value Pluralism
definition: The view that multiple genuine values exist and may conflict without always being reducible to one common scale or a single overriding principle.
my_take: I find this more realistic than imagining alignment as discovering one hidden utility function for humanity. Safety may require institutions that can negotiate and protect plural values, not merely models that optimize a compressed average of them.
why_it_matters: AI systems deployed across societies will encounter conflicts among autonomy, welfare, equality, freedom, truth, privacy, and other values that cannot always be simultaneously maximized.

sources.title: Value Theory
sources.importance: Overview of monism, pluralism, and value comparison

name: Population Ethics
definition: The branch of ethics concerned with how to compare outcomes involving different numbers of people, different identities, and different distributions of well-being across possible populations.
my_take: This is where apparently simple statements about 'benefiting humanity' become surprisingly unstable. The answer can change depending on whether we prioritize average welfare, total welfare, rights, equality, or the claims of possible future people.
why_it_matters: Claims about existential risk and the value of the long-term future often depend on assumptions about future populations that are philosophically contested rather than technically given.

sources.title: The Repugnant Conclusion
sources.authors: Gustaf Arrhenius, Jesper Ryberg, Torbjörn Tännsjö

name: Longtermism
definition: The view that positively influencing the long-term future is an important moral priority, with stronger versions claiming it should be among the most important priorities of the present.
my_take: I agree that future people matter and that irreversible choices deserve unusual attention. I am less convinced that this automatically tells us which present institutions, technologies, or interventions deserve priority, especially under deep empirical and moral uncertainty.
why_it_matters: Many arguments for prioritizing advanced-AI risk derive much of their force from the scale and duration of possible effects on the future.

sources.title: The Case for Strong Longtermism

name: Intergenerational Justice
definition: The study of what present generations owe to future generations, including duties concerning resources, institutions, risk, opportunity, and the conditions under which future people can exercise agency.
my_take: I prefer this framing when longtermism becomes too abstract. It keeps the future connected to justice: who creates the risk, who receives the benefit, whose choices become constrained, and what institutions we leave behind.
why_it_matters: AI decisions made now may shape infrastructure, power, norms, and risks for people who cannot participate in present governance.

sources.title: Intergenerational Justice

name: Democratic Legitimacy
definition: The justified authority of a decision or institution grounded in fair participation, representation, accountability, transparency, lawful procedure, or public reasons acceptable to those subject to it.
my_take: An aligned system is not automatically legitimate. Even technically excellent choices about values, access, or acceptable risk can remain politically illegitimate when they are made by a small group without meaningful authority from the people affected.
why_it_matters: AI governance and value specification determine social rules at scale. Expertise is necessary, but expertise alone does not answer who has the right to decide.

sources.title: Legitimacy of Algorithmic Decision-Making: Six Threats and the Need for a Calibrated Institutional Response
Toward Democracy Levels for AI
sources.importance: Framework for evaluating democratic AI processes

name: Distributional Harm
definition: Harm that is unevenly allocated across people or groups, including cases where aggregate benefits conceal concentrated burdens, exclusion, reduced opportunity, or unequal exposure to risk.
my_take: Average benefit is not enough. I want to know who absorbs model error, whose labor is displaced, whose data is used, who is surveilled, and who receives the power created by the system.
why_it_matters: AI systems can improve overall performance while systematically worsening outcomes for smaller, less represented, or less powerful populations.

sources.title: Fairness and Abstraction in Sociotechnical Systems
sources.importance: Foundational sociotechnical critique of fairness abstraction

name: Sentience
definition: The capacity to have subjective experiences with a felt quality, especially experiences that can be positively or negatively valenced, such as pleasure, pain, comfort, or distress.
my_take: I separate sentience from intelligence, language, agency, and self-report. A system could be highly capable without experience, while a much less capable system could still matter morally if there is something it is like to be that system.
why_it_matters: If artificial systems ever become sentient, their treatment could create a new class of moral harms that ordinary alignment frameworks are not designed to detect.

sources.title: The Search for Invertebrate Consciousness

name: Machine Consciousness
definition: The possibility that an artificial system could possess subjective experience or other properties associated with consciousness, rather than merely producing behavior that humans interpret as conscious.
my_take: I do not think fluent self-report is evidence enough, and I do not think digital implementation rules consciousness out. The honest position is that the science of consciousness is unsettled, so claims in either direction need more than intuition.
why_it_matters: Conscious AI would affect moral status, training practices, model deletion, replication, labor, consent, and the ethics of creating systems capable of suffering.

sources.title: Consciousness in Artificial Intelligence: Insights from the Science of Consciousness
Could a Large Language Model Be Conscious?

name: Moral Patienthood
definition: The status of being an entity whose welfare, interests, or experiences matter morally in their own right and can ground duties in other agents.
my_take: Moral patienthood asks a different question from whether a system is responsible, intelligent, useful, or legally recognized. Something may deserve protection without being a moral agent or a person in the full political sense.
why_it_matters: Future AI policy may need to decide whether artificial systems can be harmed and what evidence is sufficient to trigger protective obligations.

sources.title: The Grounds of Moral Status
sources.importance: Overview of theories of moral standing

name: Anthropomorphism
definition: The tendency to attribute humanlike minds, motives, emotions, intentions, or social roles to nonhuman entities based on appearance, behavior, language, or interaction.
my_take: Anthropomorphism can make us overestimate consciousness and coherent agency, but refusing all human language can also hide real functional similarities. I want to treat it as a bias to examine, not a conversation-ending accusation.
why_it_matters: Language models are designed to participate in human conversation, making users especially likely to infer stable beliefs, feelings, intentions, or relationships from generated behavior.

sources.title: On Seeing Human: A Three-Factor Theory of Anthropomorphism
sources.authors: Nicholas Epley, Adam Waytz, John T. Cacioppo

name: Sociotechnical Systems
definition: Systems whose outcomes emerge from interactions among technology, people, institutions, incentives, workflows, norms, infrastructure, and power rather than from the technical artifact alone.
my_take: This may be the concept that most changes how I read AI-risk arguments. The model is never the whole system. Access rules, operators, organizations, markets, users, and governance can amplify, redirect, or contain what the model can do.
why_it_matters: Evaluating only model behavior can miss harms and safeguards that arise from deployment context, institutional structure, and human adaptation.

sources.title: Fairness and Abstraction in Sociotechnical Systems
sources.importance: Applied sociotechnical framework for AI systems

sources.title: Some Social and Psychological Consequences of the Longwall Method of Coal-Getting
sources.authors: Eric L. Trist, K. W. Bamforth

name: Normalization of Deviance
definition: The gradual process by which repeated departures from safety rules or expected performance become accepted as normal because they have not yet produced visible disaster.
my_take: This is one of the organizational risks I find most convincing. A safeguard can erode without anyone deciding to remove it; each successful exception becomes evidence that the margin was unnecessary until the system finally encounters the wrong conditions.
why_it_matters: Competitive pressure and repeated near-misses can cause AI organizations to accept weaker evaluations, broader access, or riskier deployment as ordinary practice.

sources.title: The Challenger Launch Decision: Risky Technology, Culture, and Deviance at NASA
The Parable of the Boiled System Safety Professional
